examples: No node comparison feature — unable to diff or correlate two nodes (e.g., compare `daemon.*` vs `droid.*` namespace behavior), which limits hypothesis testing during malware or obfuscated binary analysis.
No visible support for sub-techniques or tactic-level filtering (e.g., “Execution”, “Persistence”) — critical for granular MITRE mapping and adversary emulation.
Integration with “Vulnerability Map” and “DROID / EXPLORER” context implies a layered analysis workflow, useful for correlating binary features with attack surfaces and state machines.
Populate the ATT&CK column with actual technique IDs (e.g., T1059.001) and names (e.g., “PowerShell”) based on feature heuristics or signature matching — even if inferred with confidence scores.
Add a dropdown or toggle to filter by MITRE tactic (e.g., “Initial Access”, “Defense Evasion”) and enable sub-technique expansion for deeper behavioral mapping.
No indication of whether ATT&CK-to-D3FEND mapping is automated or manual — critical for reproducibility and trust in tool output.
**Add a “Code View” toggle** next to “All Features” that switches between graph and source, ensuring analysts can cross-reference features with actual code — essential for professional RE workflows.
No visible command-line or natural language input tied to graph manipulation — “show all crypto functions” or “expand auth namespace” cannot be issued via chat or voice.
Graph interaction is purely manual (click/double-click/scroll) — no bidirectional sync with chat context (e.g., clicking a node doesn’t populate a query or explain it in chat).
Lacks agentic or scriptable control — no API, CLI, or voice-to-action pipeline, which is critical for scalable, automated, or accessibility-driven RE workflows.
Metadata summary at bottom (features, edges, namespaces) provides quick statistical grounding — helpful for triage and prioritization.
Implement bidirectional context sync: clicking a node should auto-generate a chat prompt (“Explain this state machine”) and populate the query box for refinement.
False positives or uncertain matches aren’t visually distinguished — confidence is shown globally but not per-node or per-pattern, which reduces trust in automated labeling.
No obvious annotation or comment system — annotations are essential for collaborative or long-term analysis and are missing here.
Feature-rich metadata panel (T0/T1/T2, confidence, ELF stats) gives immediate context — useful for prioritizing reverse engineering effort.
Add a heatmap or color-coded node/edge overlay to indicate analysis depth (e.g., gray = unvisited, green = fully analyzed, yellow = partially analyzed).
Add a fallback response when a query can’t be answered: “I don’t know, but try asking about namespaces, state machines, or CLI commands in droid.”
Some UI text (e.g., “No nodes in scene” dialog) uses low-saturation purple/blue tones that may wash out or blend into the background under poor lighting or YouTube compression.
No visible export button or menu for Markdown, HTML, or even image snapshots — critical for writeup creation and documentation.
Graphs are rendered in-canvas with no apparent way to export as SVG/PNG or embed as Markdown image links — forces manual screenshotting and breaks workflow.
Real-time stats (features, namespaces, edges) and confidence metrics (97% conf) give immediate analytical context — useful for triage.

Add a “Export to Markdown” button that generates a ready-to-paste document with embedded graph images (as base64 or hosted links), formatted code blocks, and metadata (binary name, features, confidence).
Include “Export as HTML” with embedded interactive graph (via D3.js or similar) for embedding in blogs or internal wikis — preserves interactivity for readers.
The “6/334 in scene” indicator is cryptic — doesn’t explain what “in scene” means or how to manipulate it, which could confuse new users.
The UI implies code analysis capability but provides no actual code view — tokenization, keyword coloring, or language-specific constructs cannot be assessed because they’re not rendered.

**Implement a live code viewer pane** that renders decompiled/disassembled output with proper syntax highlighting for ASM (x86/ARM), C (structs, pointers, macros), and Python (decorators, comprehensions) — using a proven scheme like Monokai or Dracula.
Add a small “×” clear button inside the search field and a “Search History” dropdown (even if just last 3 queries) to improve ergonomics and reduce friction.
No visible rename field, hotkey, or context menu option for renaming nodes — critical for RE workflow where function/variable renaming is constant.
Add a double-click-to-rename or F2 hotkey on graph nodes, with inline edit field and immediate sync across graph/detail panels.
Feature tagging (rpc, event, schema, etc.) and color-coded legend enable quick semantic filtering — useful for identifying attack surfaces or protocol boundaries.
No visible assembly or source code view — critical for teaching how high-level constructs map to machine instructions.
Lacks interactive disassembly or instruction-level tracing — essential for understanding control flow and vulnerabilities.
Add a “Source ↔ ASM” toggle or split-pane view that links high-level constructs (e.g., Python functions) to their compiled assembly, with line-to-line highlighting.
The “Ask about this binary…” field lacks autocomplete, examples, or a “Did you mean?” feature, which could help beginners rephrase failed queries.
The chat interaction appears to be a one-way prompt — no history, no ability to edit or retry a question inline, which may frustrate users who mistype or ask poorly formed questions.
The “Ask about this binary…” input field is clearly labeled and positioned, encouraging exploration without overwhelming the user.
The UI includes visual cues (color-coded node types, stats, and namespace breakdowns) that help users self-correct or reframe questions based on what’s visible.

Add a “Compare Selected Nodes” button or drag-drop comparison mode to enable side-by-side feature/behavior analysis — critical for detecting code reuse, obfuscation patterns, or malicious intent across namespaces.
Add a “Mitigation Plan” generator button that auto-creates a playbook from selected features, including D3FEND controls, detection rules, and hardening steps — turning analysis into action.

Color scheme, while dark, is inconsistent in its application — purple highlights for selected binaries, green for “deploy”, but no coherent semantic coloring for code elements (e.g., registers, syscalls, strings).
Add a persistent “Command Chat” panel with NLP-to-graph-action mapping (e.g., “show functions in daemon.* namespace” → auto-filters + highlights).
No visible highlighting or labeling of low-level binary patterns (vtable, PLT/GOT, exception handlers, string tables, relocations) — these are foundational in RE and absent from the UI.

Add a “Low-Level Patterns” toggle or tab that explicitly labels and highlights vtables, PLT/GOT entries, exception handlers, and string tables — with color-coding for confidence (e.g., green = high, yellow = medium, red = uncertain).
Xref lists (if they exist) are not visibly clickable or exposed; no indication of incoming/outgoing references for selected nodes.

Make all xref entries (e.g., “Gy0 (add_mcp_server)”) clickable and display both incoming/outgoing edges in a side panel when a node is selected — essential for understanding control/data flow in complex binaries.
“6 in scene” is ambiguous — does it mean 6 nodes selected? 6 nodes analyzed? Without context, it’s unclear whether this reflects coverage or just current viewport selection.
No code block formatting or syntax highlighting in the analysis panel — makes sharing snippets (e.g., function signatures, ELF sections) messy for blog posts.
The ATT&CK column is empty across all rows — no technique IDs or names are populated, rendering the column useless for actual classification or reporting.

**No syntax highlighting visible at all** — the central pane labeled “SOURCE PATTERNS (0 available)” is empty, and no ASM/C/Python code is displayed for evaluation. This is a critical omission for a tool claiming to support multi-language RE.
No visual indication of unvisited or under-analyzed regions in the graph viewport — the canvas is empty, which may mislead users into thinking nothing is there, rather than “not yet loaded/selected.”
The central graph area is currently empty — while not a UI flaw per se, it leaves a large “dead zone” visually that could mislead viewers into thinking the tool is unresponsive.
Add a subtle “loading” or “select a binary to begin” animation or placeholder graphic in the central graph area to guide user attention and avoid visual emptiness during recordings.
The UI lacks visual indicators (e.g., color-coding, icons, tooltips) to highlight ATT&CK relevance or confidence, which would aid rapid assessment during live analysis.
The actual context menu contents are not visible in the screenshot — so we can’t verify if options are beginner-friendly, grouped logically, or hide advanced features.
No visible “Help” or “Learn this feature” option directly tied to the right-click action — beginners may still feel lost without in-context guidance.
Add a “Show Help for This Node” or “What Can I Do Here?” option directly in the right-click context menu to guide beginners.
Replace “6/334 in scene” with a tooltip or hover explanation (e.g., “6 of 334 features currently visible — use filters or zoom to explore more”) to reduce ambiguity.
No visible “clear search” button or history — forces manual deletion or navigation to reset, breaking workflow.
No undo history or bulk rename capability — makes iterative renaming risky and inefficient.
No visible back button, navigation history, or “go to caller” functionality — critical for bidirectional call graph traversal in RE workflows.
Add a “Back” button and persistent navigation stack (like IDA’s “Jump to” history) to enable seamless traversal between callees and callers.

No visible context menu on right-click — missing essential RE actions like “Go to address”, “Copy symbol name”, “Add to watchlist”, or “Show in graph” — which are standard in professional tools like IDA, Ghidra, or Binary Ninja.
Implement a right-click context menu per node with actions: “Show in Graph”, “Copy Symbol”, “Add to Watchlist”, “Go to Address (if available)”, and “Trigger Analysis on Node”.
Introduce a right-click “Add Comment” option with persistent storage (local or cloud) and visible annotation badges on nodes — essential for team-based or long-term RE projects.
The “No nodes in scene” state is unhelpful without context — it doesn’t guide the user toward loading meaningful symbols or sections (e.g., .plt, .got, .rodata).
No progress indicator for ongoing analysis (e.g., “analyzing 42% of functions…”), which is critical when dealing with large binaries or long-running static analysis passes.
Introduce a progress bar or status badge for active analysis tasks (e.g., “Analyzing functions… 78% complete”) and clarify “6 in scene” with a tooltip or legend.
No indication whether the search failed due to zero matches vs. system error — ambiguous state.
There’s no visible error handling or feedback mechanism if a user asks an unanswerable question — no “I don’t know” or “Try asking about X” message is shown in the screenshot.
No side-by-side comparison or cross-referencing between source and ASM — fails to teach compiler behavior or optimization.
Integrate a “Compiler Explorer”-style mode where users can paste source code, compile it inline, and immediately see the resulting assembly with annotations — ideal for teaching compiler behavior.
Navigation context (selected node, zoom level, viewport) is not preserved — if you drill into a namespace or function, returning to prior state requires manual re-navigation.

weaknesses: No visible context menu on right-click — missing essential RE actions like “Go to address”, “Copy symbol name”, “Add to watchlist”, or “Show in graph” — which are standard in professional tools like IDA, Ghidra, or Binary Ninja.

No node comparison feature — unable to diff or correlate two nodes (e.g., compare `daemon.*` vs `droid.*` namespace behavior), which limits hypothesis testing during malware or obfuscated binary analysis.
suggestions: Implement a right-click context menu per node with actions: “Show in Graph”, “Copy Symbol”, “Add to Watchlist”, “Go to Address (if available)”, and “Trigger Analysis on Node”.

Add a “Compare Selected Nodes” button or drag-drop comparison mode to enable side-by-side feature/behavior analysis — critical for detecting code reuse, obfuscation patterns, or malicious intent across namespaces.
reviews.fullText:
1. PASS  
2. Score: 7/10  

**Strengths**  
- The interface provides clear namespace grouping (e.g., `daemon.*`, `droid.*`, `tunnel.*`) which aids in scoping analysis to relevant subsystems — critical for triaging large binaries.  
- The “Fuzzy search features…” bar and tag-based filtering (rpc, event, schema, etc.) enable rapid feature discovery, which is essential for agentic or exploratory reverse engineering workflows.  
- The bottom panel’s “TOP FEATURES BY CONNECTIONS” gives immediate insight into high-impact functions (e.g., `add_mcp_server`, `archive_session`), helping prioritize analysis based on connectivity and frequency.

**Weaknesses**  
- No visible context menu on right-click — missing essential RE actions like “Go to address”, “Copy symbol name”, “Add to watchlist”, or “Show in graph” — which are standard in professional tools like IDA, Ghidra, or Binary Ninja.  
- No node comparison feature — unable to diff or correlate two nodes (e.g., compare `daemon.*` vs `droid.*` namespace behavior), which limits hypothesis testing during malware or obfuscated binary analysis.  
- The “ANALYSIS” panel suggests queries but doesn’t tie them to selected nodes — e.g., “How do daemon.* and droid.* interact?” should be actionable from a selected node, not just static suggestions.

**What you would change**  
- Implement a right-click context menu per node with actions: “Show in Graph”, “Copy Symbol”, “Add to Watchlist”, “Go to Address (if available)”, and “Trigger Analysis on Node”.  
- Add a “Compare Selected Nodes” button or drag-drop comparison mode to enable side-by-side feature/behavior analysis — critical for detecting code reuse, obfuscation patterns, or malicious intent across namespaces.

No visible support for sub-techniques or tactic-level filtering (e.g., “Execution”, “Persistence”) — critical for granular MITRE mapping and adversary emulation.
The UI lacks visual indicators (e.g., color-coding, icons, tooltips) to highlight ATT&CK relevance or confidence, which would aid rapid assessment during live analysis.

Add a dropdown or toggle to filter by MITRE tactic (e.g., “Initial Access”, “Defense Evasion”) and enable sub-technique expansion for deeper behavioral mapping.

reviews.fullText:
1. FAIL  
2. Score: 4/10

3. Strengths  
- Clean, dark-themed UI with logical panel separation (binaries, features, analysis, queries) — good for prolonged RE sessions.  
- Feature-level confidence scoring (100% conf) and taxonomy tagging (cli_command, event) provide immediate signal on feature reliability.  
- Integration of ATT&CK and D3FEND columns suggests intent to bridge offensive and defensive frameworks — a valuable direction for enterprise RE tools.

4. Weaknesses  
- D3FEND column is entirely empty across all features — no countermeasures suggested, rendering the ATT&CK mapping useless for mitigation planning.  
- No indication of whether ATT&CK-to-D3FEND mapping is automated or manual — critical for reproducibility and trust in tool output.  
- Suggested queries are generic and lack context-specific mitigation guidance (e.g., “What does request_permission do?” doesn’t translate to “Block this via D3FEND D3-AC-001”).

5. What you would change  
- Populate D3FEND column with actual mitigations per ATT&CK technique (e.g., if “T1078 Valid Accounts” is mapped, suggest “D3-AC-001: Account Management” or “D3-AC-003: Privileged Account Management”).  
- Add a “Mitigation Plan” generator button that auto-creates a playbook from selected features, including D3FEND controls, detection rules, and hardening steps — turning analysis into action.

reviews.fullText:
1. PASS  
2. Score: 7/10

3. Strengths:
- The tool surfaces high-level semantic features (namespaces, state machines, CLI commands) which is valuable for rapid triage and understanding binary intent.
- Confidence metrics (e.g., “conf: 97%”) are displayed — this is critical for professional RE workflows where certainty matters.
- Integration of source pattern browsing (ASM/decompiled C/Python pseudocode) suggests strong cross-referencing capability, which aids in bridging low-level and high-level analysis.

4. Weaknesses:
- No visible highlighting or labeling of low-level binary patterns (vtable, PLT/GOT, exception handlers, string tables, relocations) — these are foundational in RE and absent from the UI.
- The “No nodes in scene” state is unhelpful without context — it doesn’t guide the user toward loading meaningful symbols or sections (e.g., .plt, .got, .rodata).
- False positives or uncertain matches aren’t visually distinguished — confidence is shown globally but not per-node or per-pattern, which reduces trust in automated labeling.

5. What you would change:
- Add a “Low-Level Patterns” toggle or tab that explicitly labels and highlights vtables, PLT/GOT entries, exception handlers, and string tables — with color-coding for confidence (e.g., green = high, yellow = medium, red = uncertain).
- When “No nodes in scene,” auto-suggest loading common sections (e.g., “Try loading .plt or .rodata to see function pointers or strings”) — reduce friction for new users and reinforce RE best practices.

- Clear coverage metrics: “334 features · 755 edges” and “6/334 in scene” give immediate insight into analysis scope and current focus.
- Namespace and feature categorization (e.g., cli command, state machine, rpc) provides semantic context critical for RE workflow.

4. Weaknesses:
- No visual indication of unvisited or under-analyzed regions in the graph viewport — the canvas is empty, which may mislead users into thinking nothing is there, rather than “not yet loaded/selected.”
- No progress indicator for ongoing analysis (e.g., “analyzing 42% of functions…”), which is critical when dealing with large binaries or long-running static analysis passes.
- “6 in scene” is ambiguous — does it mean 6 nodes selected? 6 nodes analyzed? Without context, it’s unclear whether this reflects coverage or just current viewport selection.

5. What you would change:
- Add a heatmap or color-coded node/edge overlay to indicate analysis depth (e.g., gray = unvisited, green = fully analyzed, yellow = partially analyzed).
- Introduce a progress bar or status badge for active analysis tasks (e.g., “Analyzing functions… 78% complete”) and clarify “6 in scene” with a tooltip or legend.

weaknesses: No visible assembly or source code view — critical for teaching how high-level constructs map to machine instructions.
No side-by-side comparison or cross-referencing between source and ASM — fails to teach compiler behavior or optimization.
Lacks interactive disassembly or instruction-level tracing — essential for understanding control flow and vulnerabilities.
suggestions: Add a “Source ↔ ASM” toggle or split-pane view that links high-level constructs (e.g., Python functions) to their compiled assembly, with line-to-line highlighting.
Integrate a “Compiler Explorer”-style mode where users can paste source code, compile it inline, and immediately see the resulting assembly with annotations — ideal for teaching compiler behavior.

1. FAIL

3. Strengths

- Rich metadata display (namespaces, features, edges) helps users grasp binary structure at a high level.  
- Suggested queries panel encourages exploratory learning — useful for CTF-style guided discovery.  

4. Weaknesses  
- No visible assembly or source code view — critical for teaching how high-level constructs map to machine instructions.  
- No side-by-side comparison or cross-referencing between source and ASM — fails to teach compiler behavior or optimization.  
- Lacks interactive disassembly or instruction-level tracing — essential for understanding control flow and vulnerabilities.  

5. What you would change

weaknesses: There’s no visible error handling or feedback mechanism if a user asks an unanswerable question — no “I don’t know” or “Try asking about X” message is shown in the screenshot.
The “Ask about this binary…” field lacks autocomplete, examples, or a “Did you mean?” feature, which could help beginners rephrase failed queries.
The chat interaction appears to be a one-way prompt — no history, no ability to edit or retry a question inline, which may frustrate users who mistype or ask poorly formed questions.
suggestions: The “Ask about this binary…” input field is clearly labeled and positioned, encouraging exploration without overwhelming the user.
The UI includes visual cues (color-coded node types, stats, and namespace breakdowns) that help users self-correct or reframe questions based on what’s visible.
Add a fallback response when a query can’t be answered: “I don’t know, but try asking about namespaces, state machines, or CLI commands in droid.”
reviews.fullText:
1. PASS  
2. Score: 8/10

3. Strengths  
- The interface provides “Suggested Queries” that are contextually relevant to the binary (e.g., “How do daemon.* namespace and droid.* namespace interact?”), which gently guides beginners toward productive questions.  
- The “Ask about this binary…” input field is clearly labeled and positioned, encouraging exploration without overwhelming the user.  
- The UI includes visual cues (color-coded node types, stats, and namespace breakdowns) that help users self-correct or reframe questions based on what’s visible.

4. Weaknesses  
- There’s no visible error handling or feedback mechanism if a user asks an unanswerable question — no “I don’t know” or “Try asking about X” message is shown in the screenshot.  
- The “Ask about this binary…” field lacks autocomplete, examples, or a “Did you mean?” feature, which could help beginners rephrase failed queries.  
- The chat interaction appears to be a one-way prompt — no history, no ability to edit or retry a question inline, which may frustrate users who mistype or ask poorly formed questions.

5. What you would change  
- Add a fallback response when a query can’t be answered: “I don’t know, but try asking about namespaces, state machines, or CLI commands in droid.”  
- Implement a “Query Assistant” dropdown under the input field that suggests rephrasings or related concepts based on the binary’s features (e.g., “Try: ‘What does request_permission do?’” if the user types “what is permission?”).

weaknesses: Some UI text (e.g., “No nodes in scene” dialog) uses low-saturation purple/blue tones that may wash out or blend into the background under poor lighting or YouTube compression.
suggestions: The central graph area is currently empty — while not a UI flaw per se, it leaves a large “dead zone” visually that could mislead viewers into thinking the tool is unresponsive.
Add a subtle “loading” or “select a binary to begin” animation or placeholder graphic in the central graph area to guide user attention and avoid visual emptiness during recordings.
reviews.fullText:
1. PASS  
2. Score: 8/10  

3. Strengths  
- High contrast between text and background in most panels (especially left sidebar and bottom metadata), making it legible even under screen capture compression.  
- Interactive elements (buttons like “Load Sample Graph”, “View Vulnerability Map”) use distinct color fills and icons, making them visually separable from static text.  
- Dark theme is well-balanced — not too dark to lose detail, with subtle gradients and highlights that preserve depth without crushing blacks.  

4. Weaknesses  
- Some UI text (e.g., “No nodes in scene” dialog) uses low-saturation purple/blue tones that may wash out or blend into the background under poor lighting or YouTube compression.  
- Suggested queries in the right “ANALYSIS” panel use cyan-on-dark that, while stylish, risks low readability for viewers with color vision deficiencies or on low-end displays.  
- The central graph area is currently empty — while not a UI flaw per se, it leaves a large “dead zone” visually that could mislead viewers into thinking the tool is unresponsive.  

5. What you would change  
- Increase saturation or add subtle stroke/border to key UI text (e.g., dialog headers, suggested queries) to ensure legibility under video compression.  
- Add a subtle “loading” or “select a binary to begin” animation or placeholder graphic in the central graph area to guide user attention and avoid visual emptiness during recordings.

weaknesses: No visible export button or menu for Markdown, HTML, or even image snapshots — critical for writeup creation and documentation.
Graphs are rendered in-canvas with no apparent way to export as SVG/PNG or embed as Markdown image links — forces manual screenshotting and breaks workflow.
No code block formatting or syntax highlighting in the analysis panel — makes sharing snippets (e.g., function signatures, ELF sections) messy for blog posts.
suggestions: Real-time stats (features, namespaces, edges) and confidence metrics (97% conf) give immediate analytical context — useful for triage.

Add a “Export to Markdown” button that generates a ready-to-paste document with embedded graph images (as base64 or hosted links), formatted code blocks, and metadata (binary name, features, confidence).
Include “Export as HTML” with embedded interactive graph (via D3.js or similar) for embedding in blogs or internal wikis — preserves interactivity for readers.

1. FAIL

3. Strengths  
- Clean, dark-themed UI with intuitive node/edge visualization for binary feature graphs — excellent for live debugging and CTF-style exploration.  
- Suggested queries panel provides quick-start analysis prompts (e.g., “What does request_permission do?”), which is pedagogically valuable for learners and researchers.  
- Real-time stats (features, namespaces, edges) and confidence metrics (97% conf) give immediate analytical context — useful for triage.

4. Weaknesses

- Graphs are rendered in-canvas with no apparent way to export as SVG/PNG or embed as Markdown image links — forces manual screenshotting and breaks workflow.  
- No code block formatting or syntax highlighting in the analysis panel — makes sharing snippets (e.g., function signatures, ELF sections) messy for blog posts.

5. What you would change  
- Add a “Export to Markdown” button that generates a ready-to-paste document with embedded graph images (as base64 or hosted links), formatted code blocks, and metadata (binary name, features, confidence).  
- Include “Export as HTML” with embedded interactive graph (via D3.js or similar) for embedding in blogs or internal wikis — preserves interactivity for readers.

weaknesses: The actual context menu contents are not visible in the screenshot — so we can’t verify if options are beginner-friendly, grouped logically, or hide advanced features.
No visible “Help” or “Learn this feature” option directly tied to the right-click action — beginners may still feel lost without in-context guidance.
The “6/334 in scene” indicator is cryptic — doesn’t explain what “in scene” means or how to manipulate it, which could confuse new users.
suggestions: Add a “Show Help for This Node” or “What Can I Do Here?” option directly in the right-click context menu to guide beginners.
Replace “6/334 in scene” with a tooltip or hover explanation (e.g., “6 of 334 features currently visible — use filters or zoom to explore more”) to reduce ambiguity.
reviews.fullText:
1. PASS  
2. Score: 7/10

3. Strengths  
- The interface clearly labels interaction modes (click, double-click, right-click, scroll, drag) in the header — this is excellent for onboarding and reduces guesswork.  
- The right-click context menu is custom (not browser default), and the presence of “context menu” in the tooltip implies intentional design for RE workflows.  
- Suggested queries in the ANALYSIS panel act as a “What can I do here?” guide — helpful for beginners to discover tool capabilities without trial-and-error.

4. Weaknesses  
- The actual context menu contents are not visible in the screenshot — so we can’t verify if options are beginner-friendly, grouped logically, or hide advanced features.  
- No visible “Help” or “Learn this feature” option directly tied to the right-click action — beginners may still feel lost without in-context guidance.  
- The “6/334 in scene” indicator is cryptic — doesn’t explain what “in scene” means or how to manipulate it, which could confuse new users.

5. What you would change  
- Add a “Show Help for This Node” or “What Can I Do Here?” option directly in the right-click context menu to guide beginners.  
- Replace “6/334 in scene” with a tooltip or hover explanation (e.g., “6 of 334 features currently visible — use filters or zoom to explore more”) to reduce ambiguity.
